title: There’s a lot of hype around perimenopause. Don’t buy it.
gist: Menopause specialists say the social-media-driven perimenopause boom has outrun the science: there is no valid hormone test for perimenopause, hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is being pushed on perimenopausal women it was never trialed or approved for, and much of the supplement market targeting these women lacks evidence or even the labeled active ingredient.

themes.theme: No valid diagnostic test exists for perimenopause

insights.claim: There is no valid blood test for perimenopause because hormone levels (estrogen, progesterone, luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone) fluctuate wildly and unpredictably during this stage before leveling off after menopause, according to former International Menopause Society president Mary Ann Lumsden.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Directly undercuts the premise of consumer diagnostic products (blood tests, hormone-tracking apps) being marketed for perimenopause detection, a reputational and regulatory risk flag for femtech/diagnostics startups in this space.
insights.quote: You can’t interpret hormone [measures] because they change so much... And that is quite normal.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "That’s because hormones can fluctuate wildly"

insights.claim: Perimenopause symptoms are typically at their worst during this pre-menopause phase itself, not after menopause is reached, per Mary Ann Lumsden.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Clinically relevant baseline fact for any product or service targeting the menopause-adjacent health market, since it defines when patient demand/pain is highest.
insights.quote: Often symptoms are at their worst in the perimenopause
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Perimenopause tends to start around"

themes.theme: HRT is being oversold to perimenopausal women beyond its evidence base

insights.claim: Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) was trialed and approved for menopausal women, not perimenopausal women, and giving standard HRT to a perimenopausal woman risks having it 'swamped' by her own ongoing hormone production, plus a risk of abnormal bleeding, according to menopause specialists Mary Ann Lumsden and Paula Briggs.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A clinical mismatch between drug label population (menopausal) and the population increasingly seeking prescriptions off-narrative (perimenopausal) via social media pressure — relevant liability/off-label-use exposure for telehealth HRT prescribers and menopause-focused digital health startups.
insights.quote: If you give standard HRT, it may well get swamped by [the woman’s] own hormone production... HRT can also cause abnormal bleeding in perimenopausal women
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "But these drugs were trialed in"

insights.claim: Social media is heavily promoting the message that perimenopausal women should demand HRT and switch doctors if refused, a pattern former British Menopause Society chair Paula Briggs calls 'almost cult-like' and says is being pushed particularly hard onto younger women who assume they are perimenopausal.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Identifies social-media-driven demand inflation as a distinct commercial force in women's health, useful context for evaluating growth claims of menopause-focused telehealth and content platforms whose growth may be running ahead of clinical appropriateness.
insights.quote: It’s almost cult-like, this idea that everybody must have HRT
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "HRT is essentially designed to top up"

themes.theme: Supplement market lacks evidence and quality control

insights.claim: Marketed vitamins and supplements targeted at middle-aged and menopausal women have little to no supporting evidence, and Lumsden says blood tests on patients who reported taking 'testosterone' supplements showed no corresponding rise in testosterone levels, implying the products did not contain what they claimed.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A concrete red flag on product-quality and labeling integrity in the fast-growing menopause supplement category, relevant for due diligence on consumer-health/supplement brands riding the perimenopause trend.
insights.quote: I can’t see a mechanism for a lot of them... Whatever they’re getting, it’s not testosterone.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "And then there are the supplements"

themes.theme: Overattribution of general midlife symptoms to hormones

insights.claim: Broad symptom lists circulating on social media (fatigue, brain fog, aches and pains, digestive issues) do not closely track actual menstrual-cycle and hormone changes across the menopause transition, according to University of Colorado menopause researcher Nanette Santoro, who says attributing all unpleasant midlife symptoms to perimenopause has no scientific basis.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Suggests the addressable-market narrative for perimenopause-branded products may be overstated by conflating unrelated midlife stressors (caregiving, work) with a hormonal condition, worth discounting broad market-sizing claims in this space.
insights.quote: Attributing everything unpleasant that happens to a woman over 35 to perimenopause is not based on any scientific evidence.
